references.title: Student Profiles of Change in a University Course: A Complex Dynamical Systems Perspective

extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The LMS logs were categorized into different types of SRL constructs. 

Data is collected every week, so essencially we have a sequence of different behaviors as the input.
extracted.Cluster Description:
Students with Stable Weekly Study Patterns (n=158) - entropy of recurrence in weekly activities remained stable and high throughout the course.

Students with Steep Chances in Weekly Study Patterns (n=40) - highest entropy of recurrence at the start of the course.  These students were the ones with the lowest performance on the course.

Students with Moderate Changes in Study Patterns (n=90): Slight decrease in recurrence over time.
extracted.Implications:
All groups of students modified behavior when closer to the exam.

Highly disordered behaviour of students in early weeks could be used as a warning sign for students who may struggle.
extracted.Comments:
This study groups sequences of behaviors and identifies the performance of different segments of students. 

Authors find that students who display a more erratic behavior at the start of the course tend to perform worse than their peers.
extracted.Limitations:
Based exclusively on one course.

Measure is sensible to window size. ptimizing for window size may be an interesting approach,

annotations.text: Integrating temporal patterns into descriptive analytics profiling works is often underexplored. The authors address this issue by evaluating how study profiles evolve in a blended-learning undergraduate course. Adjustments to the student profile were done using clickstream data.
Authors collect student trajectories and cluster them. 3 patterns were identified:
Learners with steep changes achieved lower performances on exam. The authors also tie back their results to existing SRL theory.
